metadata.description: C&C 2026 poster submission-prep pass: rewrote abstract + §1 opening around extraction/displacement paradox framing, added methodological caveats (embedding prior, MLP out-of-distribution, κ reframing), added Limitations paragraph, trimmed over-spec'd methods (MLP architecture, UMAP hyperparameters), replaced 'artist voice' mass-noun with 'probes' where countable, bumped figure fonts, produced docx version for Google Drive collaborative editing with revisions colored blue. Retrospective session — I did not start one at conversation start; this wraps up the work done in this one sitting.

summary:
ACCOMPLISHED: Rewrote C&C 2026 poster opening around extraction/displacement paradox; added methodological caveats (embedding prior, MLP OOD, κ reframing) + Limitations paragraph; replaced 'artist voice' mass-noun with 'probes' where countable; trimmed over-spec'd methods; bumped figure fonts; produced poster_content_revised.docx with 19 changes colored blue for Google Drive review. Word count 2,714/3,000. Canonical numbers preserved.

NEXT SESSION (open items):
1. Human-LLM topic-label agreement numbers still need to be plugged into the Methods κ sentence — user confirmed this validation was done; need N probes coded, κ value, Landis-Koch range to write it in.
2. Citation for displacement claim in §1 ¶1 currently uses Lovato 2024 (the artist survey) but the claim is about illustration-labor markets + training-data litigation — needs a real source (Andersen v. Stability AI, Concept Art Association, or similar).
3. Title choice open: user asked for 10 candidates and ranking; I recommended #2 'Trained On, Compressed Out: Measuring Artist Representation in Public AI-Art Discourse (2013-2025)' vs keeping current #1 'Two Topics, 252 Artists'. No selection made yet.
4. LaTeX currently includes fig2_geometry.pdf (labeled); user asked about no-labels variant. Both have bigger fonts now. Choice still open.
5. Final-submission cleanup: strip review color markers with `sed -E 's/\\revadd\\{([^{}]*)\\}/\\1/g' main.tex` — two cases with nested \cite inside \revadd need manual fixes. Backup at main.tex.bak.20260416_183837.
6. Deadline today 2026-04-16 AoE for C&C poster; 2026-04-30 for Nature Collection (PALCOMMS) full manuscript remains primary target.
7. User announced intent to add hooks to trace-mcp for automatic tracking without explicit instructions — next session may run under that new behavior.
8. Stale session S003 (Feb 2026, 57 events, still 'active') to be cleaned up in this conversation per user request.

decision.description: Reframe the paper's opening from a soft audibility paradox ('artists' work trains the models, unclear if concerns reach public discourse') to a sharp extraction/displacement paradox ('artists' work is the substrate that trains the models, and the same models are now deployed in markets that compete with and devalue their labor'). Apply this framing to the abstract opener and rewrite §1 Motivation ¶1 to document both halves of the contradiction with concrete referents (training-data scraping, declining illustration commissions, training-data appropriation litigation), then pivot to the measurement question.
decision.rationale: The original framing stated a consequence (audibility) without naming the stake (extraction). For a C&C audience the labor-paradox framing is (a) rhetorically stronger, (b) consistent with the downstream compression finding (compression on top of extraction is a structural double erasure, not just a gap), and (c) politically appropriate to a venue sympathetic to creator-extraction framings. I offered three drafts (direct, measured, concrete) and recommended Option A.
decision.revision_note: Human picked Option A (the sharpest draft of the three AI offered) with one stylistic modification: start the abstract with 'Artists sit inside a paradox...' preserving the original cadence. Human also approved the §1 Motivation ¶1 rewrite + three cascade edits (§1 ¶3 close, §4 opening, Conclusion). Accepted: the labor-paradox framing is now the paper's rhetorical spine, reflected across abstract, motivation opener, implications opener, and conclusion.

context.conversation_snippet: User: "the paradox is that the work of artists are used to create these generative AI models (training data) but then these models are used to devalue and 'replace' artists. this is the paradox. suggest ways to improve and strengthen this opening."

annotation.content:
Pandoc 3.7.0.2 does NOT preserve LaTeX \textcolor{} or markdown bracketed-spans style='color:...' when converting to docx — both are silently stripped, producing plain-text runs with no color formatting. Workaround used here: define a \revadd{#1} LaTeX macro that emits \textcolor{blue}{[REV]#1[/REV]} (the literal bracket markers pass through pandoc as plain text), then post-process the docx with python-docx to (a) walk runs in each paragraph as a state machine, (b) toggle 'in marked region' flag on [REV]/[/REV] boundaries, (c) apply hex color to every run inside the region, (d) strip marker bytes. A marker region may span multiple docx runs when it contains nested LaTeX commands like \cite or \emph, because pandoc emits each nested command as its own run — the state machine handles this correctly. Verified end-to-end: 0 marker bytes remaining, 36 blue runs, structure intact.</content>
<parameter name="tags">["pandoc", "docx", "latex", "color", "tooling", "workaround"]

Human corrected the paper's opening framing. The original abstract + §1 paragraph 1 stated a soft audibility paradox ('their work trains the models, yet it is unclear whether their concerns reach the public discourse'). The human identified that this misses the actual paradox they intended: artists' work is used to create models, which are then deployed to devalue and displace those same artists. That sharper framing was substituted throughout the opening. The original writing understated both the rhetorical stake and the author's intended argument.

context.conversation_snippet: User: "the opening (abstract?) of the C&C application document... start with the paradox of artists in generative AI but it is not really framed the way i wanted it. the paradox is that the work of artists are used to create these generative AI models (training data) but then these models are used to devalue and 'replace' artists. this is the paradox."

contribution.description: Edits to main.tex for C&C 2026 poster: (1) abstract opening replaced with extraction/displacement paradox framing; (2) §1 Motivation ¶1 fully rewritten to document both halves of the paradox; (3) cascade addition at §1 ¶3 end ('Compression on top of extraction. The pattern is structural.'); (4) §2 Methods — added embedding-space prior caveat, MLP out-of-distribution caveat, reframed Cohen's κ as inter-LLM consistency check rather than ground-truth validation; (5) §4 Implications opening replaced with labor-paradox payoff sentence; (6) new Limitations paragraph (5 caveats: US/English, single survey source, k sensitivity, embedding priors, time-bound); (7) Conclusion: added 'The compression we measure sits on top of an extraction artists did not consent to'; (8) all noun-usage 'artist voice/voices' → 'probes' (7 instances); (9) em-dashes removed, replaced with colons/parens/semicolons; (10) trimmed MLP architecture spec and UMAP hyperparameter list (saved 12 words, moved to supplementary). Final word count 2,714 of 3,000. All changes wrapped in \revadd{} for blue coloring in the review docx and blue text in compiled PDF.

context.conversation_snippet: User: "yes, do option A, but start with 'Artists sit inside a paradox...'... also do the rewrite you recommended and the 3 cascade edits plus do the minimum viable amount for a solid publication based on the prior issues you raised to bring this to a really strong submission to C&C."

contribution.description: Initial LaTeX→docx conversion for collaborative editing on Google Drive. Used pandoc + --citeproc against references.bib, with a sed pre-pass to clean LaTeX digit-grouping '1{,}259' → '1,259' that pandoc was otherwise rendering as '1 , 259' with spaces. Output: structured docx with resolved citations (e.g., '(Author 2022)'), preserved section headings, embedded figures.

context.conversation_snippet: User: "i need you to make a docx version of the poster_content.pdf for the C&C submission so we can do edits to it together on google drive (me and ariya). do that now"

contribution.description: Review-version docx with all 19 textual changes colored blue (hex 1A5FB4) for side-by-side comparison against the original Google Drive version. Implementation: custom \revadd{} LaTeX macro emits [REV]/[/REV] text markers that pandoc preserves as plain-text runs; post-processor /tmp/colorize_revisions.py walks docx paragraphs, state-machine-toggles on markers spanning multiple runs (needed for \cite / \emph nested inside marked regions), applies w:color=1A5FB4 to enclosed runs, strips markers. Verified: 0 marker bytes remaining, 36 blue runs across 19 marked regions, structure intact.

context.conversation_snippet: User: "can you make all of these changes in a different color or something so i can easily compare it to the current version of the doc that i already have in google drive and ariya is editing?"

contribution.description: Font-size increases in the poster figure generator. Figure 1 (fig1_concentration): modest ~+2pt bumps across all text elements (bar values, topic names, x-axis label, callout box, legend, title). Figure 2 (fig2_geometry and fig2_geometry_nolabels, both produced by the same function): aggressive bumps for readability — semantic axis labels 11→16pt (+45%), topic labels 11→15pt (+36%), callout 15→17pt, title 16→18pt, legend 11→13pt. Axis labelpad also increased 10→12 for breathing room. All three regenerated PDFs (fig1_concentration.pdf, fig2_geometry.pdf, fig2_geometry_nolabels.pdf) written to the poster figures directory.

context.conversation_snippet: User: "make the text slightly bigger for figure 1 and much bigger for figure 2... it is difficult to read the text, especially figure 2 axes and the topic labels"

contribution.description: Regenerated the three poster figure PDFs (and PNG companions) with new font sizes: fig1_concentration.pdf (94.9% headline callout, horizontal bars), fig2_geometry.pdf (labeled: 'T1 · collaborator' etc.), fig2_geometry_nolabels.pdf (T-numbers only). Script run verification: top2=94.8%, top4=99.9%, empty_topics=15 — matches canonical pipeline numbers. LaTeX currently includes the labeled fig2_geometry.pdf; the question of whether to switch to the nolabels variant was left open for the human.
